utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: russell ramirez is a 45 -year-old male with past medical history significant for cad status post prior status post prior rca stent in twenty eighteen hypertension and diabetes mellitus who presents for hospital follow-up after an anterior stemi now status post drug-eluting stent and lad and newly reduced ejection fraction ejection fraction thirty five percent and moderate mitral regurgitation alright russell hi how are you doing today

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: hey document i i do n't know i'm doing alright i guess

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: just alright how's it

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: how's it been since you've had your heart attack have you been have you been doing alright

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i've been seeing you for years since i had my last heart attack in two thousand eighteen but i've been doing pretty good i ca n't believe this happened again i mean i'm doing okay i guess i just feel tired every now and then and but overall i mean i guess i feel pretty well

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay good were you able to enjoy the spring weather

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah some i mean i'm hoping now that i've had my little procedure that i'll feel better and feel like getting back out and and maybe doing some walking there is some new trails here behind the rex center and maybe get out and walk those trails

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that will be fine i know you love walking the trails i know you like looking at the flowers because i think you you plant a lot of flowers as well do n't you especially around this time

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i do some gardening around the house

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: and you know i really like photography too being able to go out and take nature pictures

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so i'm hoping to be able to go out and do that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay well we'll we'll do what we can here to get you out and going doing all those fun activities again now tell me have you had any chest pain or any shortness of breath

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no not really no chest pain or shortness of breath i've been doing some short walks right around the house so like around the block

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: but i stay pretty close to the house i've been doing some light housekeeping and i do n't know i seem to be doing okay i think

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright now tell me are you able to lay flat at night when you sleep or

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well i mean i i never have truly laid flat on my back i've always slept with two pillows which is normal for me

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so i mean i guess i really do n't have any troubles with my sleeping

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay good how about are your legs swelling up

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: nope i've always i always had skinny ankles like like i got dawn knots legs

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: well that's cute were you able to afford your medications and are you taking them as prescribed

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i've been taking my medicine i got pretty good insurance there through the plant and and so the co-pay is n't too bad

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: and i've been taking them because i do n't want my sense to close up and they told me that that to take them this you know all the time and and i've been taking them since i got out of the hospital

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay well very good i'm glad you're doing that good for you russell and and then please keep that up now tell me are you watching your salt intake and trying to change your diet

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah so when i was in the hospital they said something about my way my heart pumps now

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it it's it's a little low and i might keep fluid on my legs if i'm not careful

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: right

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: and it's gon na be hard because you know i i really do like pizza and and they told me that i'm really gon na have to watch salt and they said that there is a lot of salt and pizza

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: there is a lot of salt and pizza and you know and you're gon na have to be able to avoid all the other salty foods as well so and i know that's hard but it's very important for your heart to be able to function at it's best right and you wan na be able to get out and walk you know walk take those walks again at the park and then you know do your photography so in order to do that we're gon na have to really cut back on those okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright so why do n't we go ahead and do a quick physical exam on you here i just want to take a look at you your vital signs look good i'm glad to see you're tolerating the medication well i'm gon na go ahead and feel your neck here i do n't appreciate any jugular venous distention and there are no carotid bruits on your heart exam there is a three out of six six systolic ejection murmur it's heard at the left base but that's pretty much the same as last year so we'll continue to monitor that okay let me listen to your lungs here real quick russell your lungs are clear so good good and your extremities i do n't see any swelling or edema on your right radial artery the cath site there is clean and it's dry and intact and i do n't see any hematoma so that's good and there is a palpable rra pulse so russell i did review the results of your ekg which showed normal sinus rhythm good r wave progression and evolutionary changes which are anticipated so let's go ahead and talk about my assessment plan for you for your first diagnosis of coronary artery disease we are gon na have you continue your your aspirin eighty one milligrams daily and brilinta ninety milligrams twice daily and we're gon na have you continue on that high dose statin that atorvastatin you might call it lipitor eighty milligrams daily and then also continue on that toprol fifty milligrams daily okay and i'm also going to refer you to cardiac rehab so for you to get some education about your heart and also give you the confidence to get back exercising regularly now i know patients love the cardiac rehab program i think you will do well does that sound good to you

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that sounds good document

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright so for your second diagnosis here the newly reduced left ventricular dysfunction and moderate mitral regurgitation i think your pumping function will improve in time you know they got you to the lab quickly so i think that heart muscle is just stunned and you're very compliant you're very good with your medications and following through with those so i think it will recover so that said i want you to go ahead and continue continue your lisinopril twenty milligrams a day i do n't think you need a diuretic at this time but i do want to add aldactone twelve . five milligrams daily and then you'll need to get labs next week okay and then we're gon na repeat another echocardiogram echocardiocardiogram in about two months

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then for your hypertension your third diagnosis of hypertension i want your to take your blood pressure just like you would you know every so often and then because your blood pressures actually seem fine at this time so we will continue to monitor that and i think you will tolerate the aldactone well as well

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: alright sounds good document

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay well you take care and you have a good evening

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah you too

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: bye

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 45-year-old male with a history of CAD, prior RCA stent, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus presenting for follow-up after an anterior STEMI.
The patient reports experiencing fatigue.
The patient enjoys walking, gardening, and nature photography.
The doctor asks if the patient has experienced chest pain or shortness of breath.
The patient denies chest pain and shortness of breath.
The doctor asks if the patient is able to lay flat at night.
The patient reports sleeping with two pillows and denies sleep disturbances.
The doctor asks if the patient has lower extremity swelling.
The patient denies lower extremity swelling.
The doctor asks about medication adherence and affordability.
The patient confirms medication adherence and that the cost is manageable.
The doctor asks if the patient is watching their salt intake and diet.
Physical exam reveals no carotid bruits, clear lungs, a grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base, and a clean, intact right radial artery cath site with a palpable pulse.
The EKG shows normal sinus rhythm with good R wave progression and anticipated evolutionary changes.
Coronary artery disease
Continue aspirin 81 mg daily, Brilinta 90 mg twice daily, Lipitor 80 mg daily, and Toprol 50 mg daily.
The doctor refers the patient to cardiac rehab.
Left ventricular dysfunction and mitral regurgitation
Continue lisinopril 20 mg daily and start Aldactone 12.5 mg daily.
The doctor orders labs for next week and a repeat echocardiogram in two months.
Hypertension
The patient is instructed to continue home blood pressure monitoring.

case_description: Hospital follow-up after an anterior STEMI; history of CAD status post prior RCA stent;hypertension;diabetes mellitus
